title: Threading the Needle: India’s Path Forward with China
gist: Carnegie India's Saheb Singh Chadha argues India's post-2024 thaw with China is driven, in priority order, by border stability, economic necessity, a search for new political understanding, and U.S.-driven geopolitical pressure — not primarily by Trump-era estrangement as popular narratives claim — and concludes China will practice 'strategic breadcrumbing,' offering enough concessions to keep India pacified without conceding its core interests or sharing frontier technology.

themes.theme: The 2024 thaw and its true drivers

insights.claim: Popular discourse largely attributes the India-China thaw to Trump-era U.S. foreign policy churn and India's economic needs, but the author argues these are incomplete: interviews with Indian government, military, and industry stakeholders rank the border imperative first, ahead of economic necessity, a new political understanding, and geopolitical pressure, in that order.
insights.speaker: Saheb Singh Chadha
insights.so_what: Corrects a widely repeated market narrative (India pivoting to China because of Trump) — the actual driver ranking implies India-China normalization is far more contingent on durable border-management progress than on the state of U.S. politics, which changes the forecast for how fast or reversible the thaw is.
insights.quote: there are predominantly four imperatives guiding India’s approach to China, and they exist in an order of priority
insights.locator: Based on conversations with serving and retired stakeholders

insights.claim: India and China resumed high-level engagement after the October 2024 Modi-Xi meeting in Kazan on the sidelines of BRICS, following a 2020-2024 freeze in which India explicitly conditioned the broader relationship on progress at the disputed border.
insights.speaker: Saheb Singh Chadha
insights.so_what: Establishes the timeline anchor (Oct 2024) against which all subsequent India-China developments — trade, investment liberalization, arms diplomacy — should be read as downstream consequences.
insights.locator: Indian Prime Minister Narendra Modi visited China in August–September 2025

insights.claim: An estimated 50,000-60,000 troops remain deployed by each side in forward areas along the India-China border at what Indian stakeholders describe as quasi-wartime levels, and neither government has confirmed de-escalation has occurred despite ongoing talks.
insights.speaker: Saheb Singh Chadha
insights.so_what: The military standoff is far from resolved despite the diplomatic thaw narrative — a key risk marker: any India-China trade or investment thesis should discount for the possibility that frontline troop levels remain elevated indefinitely.
insights.locator: Currently, both sides still have an estimated 50,000–60,000 troops

insights.claim: An Indian military stakeholder who served at the strategic level of command assessed that de-escalation of the Ladakh standoff is 'hardly possible' in the current environment, because troop readiness will only fall in tandem with rebuilt strategic and political trust, which remains largely absent.
insights.speaker: Saheb Singh Chadha
insights.so_what: A direct, high-conviction assessment from a former commander that the border standoff resolution investors might expect from the political thaw is not imminent — tempers optimism about a fast normalization of India-China ties.
insights.locator: An Indian military stakeholder who served at the strategic level of command

insights.claim: China has used ambiguity in its territorial claim lines to make shifting, sometimes contradictory claims and to coerce India militarily at a time and place of Beijing's choosing; a former Indian foreign secretary argues China primarily coerces India at the land border — where it holds military superiority — to distract from its weaker position in the Indian Ocean and Bay of Bengal, and to signal displeasure at India's deepening U.S. ties.
insights.speaker: Saheb Singh Chadha
insights.so_what: Frames the land border standoff as an instrument China uses for broader strategic signaling (about the US relationship and maritime positioning) rather than a purely territorial dispute — implies border tensions will track India-US relations, not just bilateral border talks.
insights.quote: The ambiguity serves Beijing well: It allows China to escalate at the border at a time and place of its choosing.
insights.locator: Particularly, Beijing has utilized the ambiguity in its claim lines

insights.claim: Both sides have agreed to explore an 'early harvest' approach to the boundary question — settling less contentious sectors first — with China previously proposing the Sikkim sector as a starting point since it considers that boundary already fixed by the 1890 Anglo-Chinese Sikkim-Tibet Convention, a proposal India has not historically accepted.
insights.speaker: Saheb Singh Chadha
insights.so_what: The Sikkim sector is the concrete, near-term test case for whether any genuine boundary progress is being made — a specific data point to monitor for the first tangible sign of border-dispute resolution.
insights.locator: China has previously suggested the settlement of the Sikkim sector

insights.claim: Two former Indian government officials believe China is not interested in meaningfully settling the border dispute through negotiation because it believes time favors its position in the long run.
insights.speaker: Saheb Singh Chadha
insights.so_what: Undercuts the premise that boundary talks will produce a durable settlement — reinforces the paper's broader 'breadcrumbing' thesis that China will engage process without conceding substance.
insights.quote: China is not interested in settling the border dispute in any meaningful way through negotiation, and it believes that in the long run, time is on its side.
insights.locator: Lastly, according to two former Indian government officials

insights.claim: India imported roughly 56% of its electronic components from China and Hong Kong in the first half of FY2025, and since early 2025 Chinese officials have discouraged tech and equipment exports and restricted personnel transfers — Foxconn's India factory was unable to receive specialized machinery and had over 300 Chinese engineers recalled in July 2025, slowing local workforce training.
insights.speaker: Saheb Singh Chadha
insights.so_what: A concrete, ongoing supply-chain vulnerability with a direct read-through to Apple's India assembly ramp — Foxconn's inability to get Chinese engineers and machinery is a real drag on India's 'China plus one' manufacturing buildout.
insights.locator: In the first half of FY2025, India imported approximately 56 percent

insights.claim: India is over 40% import-dependent on China for six critical minerals — bismuth (85.6%), lithium (82%), silicon (76%), titanium (50.6%), tellurium (48.8%), graphite (42.4%) — and imported 93% of rare earth magnets from China in FY2024-2025; China's April 2025 export restrictions on medium-to-heavy rare earths disrupted India's automobile, electronics, defense, and renewable-energy sectors more severely than global peers.
insights.speaker: Saheb Singh Chadha
insights.so_what: Quantifies India's critical-minerals chokepoint exposure to China with sector-specific breakdowns — directly relevant to sizing supply-chain risk in Indian auto, electronics, and defense manufacturing names.
insights.locator: Though it is difficult to quantify the overall dependency

insights.claim: Even after the U.S. and China eased trade tensions in June 2025 and rare-earth magnet flows to the rest of the world loosened, Beijing continued to withhold shipments to India specifically — rejecting at least two India-bound applications and leaving thirty pending, with all 51 Indian automobile-company applications still pending as of September 2025 despite high-level diplomacy, and resumed shipments in late October 2025 came with conditions barring re-export to the U.S. or military use.
insights.speaker: Saheb Singh Chadha
insights.so_what: Evidence that China selectively weaponizes rare-earth access against India specifically (not just globally) even amid warming diplomacy — a live case study for how China calibrates economic coercion around a partner's proximity to the US, relevant to any India-exposed manufacturing or EV supply-chain position.
insights.locator: It was reported in September 2025 that all fifty-one applications

insights.claim: India's five-year (2020-2024) tightening of Chinese investment screening under 'Press Note 3' reportedly cost Indian manufacturers $15 billion and 100,000 jobs, and net FDI into India fell to a record low of $353 million in FY2024-2025 versus $43.9 billion in FY2020-2021, prompting a March 2026 partial relaxation allowing non-controlling Chinese stakes without prior approval in some sectors.
insights.speaker: Saheb Singh Chadha
insights.so_what: A quantified cost of India's China-investment restrictions and the policy reversal now underway — signals a coming re-opening of Chinese capital into Indian manufacturing that changes the competitive and ownership calculus for import-substitution plays.
insights.locator: Cut to the present, and this move reportedly cost Indian manufacturers $15 billion

insights.claim: Indian firms are pursuing technology tie-ups with Chinese leaders in EV-adjacent sectors: Adani explored a battery partnership with BYD and visited Chinese solar/wind manufacturers; Ashok Leyland signed a twenty-year deal with China's CALB Group to import lithium-ion cells before building domestic design and manufacturing capability; but Reliance's planned stake in Xiamen Hithium collapsed in January 2026 when the Chinese firm withdrew amid Beijing's technology-transfer restrictions.
insights.speaker: Saheb Singh Chadha
insights.so_what: Names the specific Indian conglomerates and Chinese battery/EV partners active in cross-border tie-ups, plus a concrete deal failure (Reliance-Hithium) showing China's technology-transfer reluctance is already killing deals — a stock-specific watch list for India-China battery/EV supply-chain plays.
insights.locator: In August 2025, Bloomberg reported that the Adani Group was exploring a tie-up

insights.claim: China's preference in these joint ventures is structural: Beijing wants access to India's market without transferring technology or IP, discouraging automakers from India-based manufacturing investment in favor of exporting China-made knocked-down kits for local assembly, since Chinese companies face declining domestic consumption, deflation, and shrinking export markets from U.S. tariffs and are hunting for new outlets.
insights.speaker: Saheb Singh Chadha
insights.so_what: The core asymmetry investors should price into any India-China industrial tie-up: China needs India as a market/export outlet amid its own overcapacity, but structurally resists real technology transfer — limiting the upside case for India as a genuine tech-transfer beneficiary of the thaw.
insights.quote: the CCP wants more access to India’s markets, without enabling the technological and economic rise of a competitor.
insights.locator: In a nutshell, the CCP wants more access to India’s markets

insights.claim: The old India-China modus vivendi, built between 1988 and the early 2000s on the premise that both were developing states needing a stable environment to rise, began breaking down after 2008 as China's foreign policy turned more assertive post-financial-crisis and as India deepened U.S. security ties, prompting Chinese gray-zone coercion at the border culminating in the 2020 Galwan crisis.
insights.speaker: Saheb Singh Chadha
insights.so_what: Explains the root cause of the 2020-2024 freeze as a structural breakdown tied to India's US alignment, not a standalone border dispute — meaning any renewed deepening of India-US security ties carries a real risk of reigniting Chinese border coercion.
insights.locator: these understandings began to break down from 2008 onward

insights.claim: Deep strategic distrust persists at all levels of the Indian government and armed forces, particularly intensified by China's real-time, tactically accurate military support to Pakistan during the May 2025 India-Pakistan conflict, which one stakeholder termed 'collusion.'
insights.speaker: Saheb Singh Chadha
insights.so_what: China-Pakistan military coordination during a live India-Pakistan conflict is a hard trust-breaker that Indian planners now treat as a baseline risk in any future Pakistan crisis — a key geopolitical tail risk for South Asia stability.
insights.locator: The India-Pakistan conflict in May 2025

themes.theme: Imperative 4: Geopolitical pressure from U.S.-China dynamics

insights.claim: An Indian government stakeholder said that by July 2025 they had expected the second Trump administration to cooperate with India on China as in Trump's first term, but instead concluded India would likely need to 'fight its own battles' with China 'alone,' a concern reinforced by Trump's late-2025 description of his meeting with Xi as a meeting of the 'G2.'
insights.speaker: Saheb Singh Chadha
insights.so_what: Direct evidence that Indian policymakers now discount U.S. backing against China more than in Trump's first term — a structural risk factor for India's Indo-Pacific security posture and a reason India is hedging toward direct China engagement.
insights.locator: The stakeholder then observed that U.S.-India and U.S.-China relations

insights.claim: China sought a guarantee from India in October 2025 that rare-earth magnets exported from China would only be used for India's domestic needs and not re-exported to the United States, illustrating how a Taiwan-crisis-style U.S.-China conflict scenario would directly constrain India's supply chains and force Delhi into diplomatic balancing.
insights.speaker: Saheb Singh Chadha
insights.so_what: A concrete precedent showing China already treats India as a potential transshipment risk to the US on strategic materials — expect further end-use restrictions on Chinese exports to India as U.S.-China tension escalates.
insights.locator: China sought a guarantee from India in October 2025

insights.claim: Despite Indian fears of U.S. disengagement, the Trump administration's November 2025 National Security Strategy and 2026 National Defense Strategy explicitly affirm continued Indo-Pacific focus and describe India's role as 'indispensable,' with Under Secretary of War Elbridge Colby telling NATO allies the U.S. wants to redirect focus toward deterring China in the Indo-Pacific while allies handle more of their own conventional defense.
insights.speaker: Saheb Singh Chadha
insights.so_what: A counterweight to the India-alone narrative: official US strategy documents still prioritize India's Indo-Pacific role, suggesting the US-India security relationship is more resilient than informal Indian anxieties imply.
insights.locator: Colby noted during his visit to New Delhi in March 2026

insights.claim: China views the deep India-U.S. relationship as Washington using India to contain China in the Indo-Pacific and bring bloc confrontation to Asia, rather than as India pursuing independent national interests, and China's official statements reference only a 'multipolar world,' pointedly never a 'multipolar Asia' as India's statements do.
insights.speaker: Saheb Singh Chadha
insights.so_what: The rhetorical gap ('multipolar world' vs 'multipolar Asia') is a precise, trackable signal of China's refusal to accept India as a peer power within Asia — a subtle marker for gauging whether China's stance toward India is genuinely softening.
insights.quote: China-India relations have their own historical logic and internal driving force; their relationship does not target any third party, nor should it be disrupted by any third party.
insights.locator: During Jaishankar’s visit to China in July 2025

insights.claim: A former senior Indian official said China is aware of India's shrinking strategic space from shifting U.S. foreign policy and is content to 'let India stew'; two other government stakeholders (one serving, one retired) characterized China's India policy as containment, though the author prefers the term 'counterbalancing' since India doesn't occupy as central a place in China's strategic calculus as true containment would imply.
insights.speaker: Saheb Singh Chadha
insights.so_what: Clarifies the precise nature of China's India strategy — opportunistic pressure and patience rather than an all-out containment campaign — useful for calibrating how much urgency to assign to China-India friction versus, e.g., China-Taiwan friction.
insights.locator: A former senior Indian government official noted

insights.claim: China is already India's second-largest trading partner with $127.7 billion in two-way commerce in FY2024-2025, and Chinese customs data show bilateral trade surged to an all-time high of $155.62 billion in 2025 with a record $116.12 billion trade deficit against India — while Chinese officials simultaneously restrict technology and skilled-personnel outflows to India to protect domestic jobs and prevent investor exit.
insights.speaker: Saheb Singh Chadha
insights.so_what: India's trade deficit with China is widening even as the political relationship warms — a hard economic fact that undercuts any thesis of a rebalanced or more reciprocal India-China trade relationship in the near term.
insights.locator: As per Chinese customs data from January 2026

insights.claim: China convened its first Central Conference on Work Related to Neighboring Countries in twelve years in April 2025, signaling elevated priority on periphery stability as China navigates U.S. competition, and Foreign Minister Wang Yi's December 2025 keynote explicitly linked stabilizing China's neighborhood (including India) to managing the broader shift in the global order.
insights.speaker: Saheb Singh Chadha
entities: Central Conference on Work Related to Neighboring Countries
insights.so_what: China's India outreach is explicitly instrumental — a byproduct of needing a stable periphery while it manages U.S. competition — implying the thaw's durability is contingent on the state of U.S.-China relations, not a standalone strategic reassessment of India.
insights.locator: In April 2025, China convened the Central Conference

insights.claim: China retains clear military superiority over India at the disputed border with a larger, more modernized, better-resourced force, and views the border as a continental front in its Indo-Pacific competition with the U.S., linking India's border infrastructure buildup to a perceived U.S. strategy of containment.
insights.speaker: Saheb Singh Chadha
insights.so_what: China's persistent military edge at the border means Beijing has little incentive to cede ground in negotiations — reinforces that any near-term boundary settlement will favor Chinese terms.
insights.locator: While India has increased deployment, accelerated infrastructure buildup

themes.theme: Strategic breadcrumbing: the paper's central forecast

insights.claim: The paper's core prediction is that China will practice 'strategic breadcrumbing' — conciliating India politically, economically, and at the border just enough to prevent it from deepening its U.S. partnership, without any fundamental change in Beijing's underlying assumptions about India or compromise on its core interests.
insights.speaker: Saheb Singh Chadha
insights.so_what: The single highest-value takeaway of the paper: treat India-China 'normalization' headlines as tactical and reversible rather than a genuine strategic reset, and price India-China commercial and diplomatic deals as likely to remain shallow and contingent.
insights.quote: For the foreseeable future, China will likely conciliate with India in the political, economic, and security domains, without compromising on China’s core interests.
insights.locator: The Most Likely Scenario: Strategic Breadcrumbing

insights.claim: A stakeholder described Chinese deliberations as literally weighing how much to 'squeeze' India and how long a 'rope' to extend — a calibrated-pressure approach consistent with the breadcrumbing framework rather than either full hostility or full accommodation.
insights.speaker: Saheb Singh Chadha
insights.so_what: Confirms from an insider source that China's India policy is a deliberately tunable pressure dial, not a fixed stance — implies India-China tension could ratchet up or down quickly depending on Beijing's read of the US relationship.
insights.locator: As a stakeholder noted, the Chinese are deliberating the questions

insights.claim: It is a 'misnomer' to expect a full 'return to normalcy' in India-China relations; instead, a new normal is emerging in which India retains deep distrust of China at all government and military levels even as both sides seek to keep border tensions from re-escalating and India works to reduce economic and technological dependencies over a multi-year horizon.
insights.speaker: Saheb Singh Chadha
insights.so_what: Sets the right frame for interpreting future India-China headlines: expect a permanently more guarded, de-risking relationship rather than a reversion to pre-2020 warmth — informs a multi-year underweight on any thesis of full India-China economic re-integration.
insights.quote: It is a misnomer to expect a “return to normalcy” in the India-China relationship. A new normal is emerging.
insights.locator: Reflections

insights.claim: Terrain asymmetry means re-mobilization costs are structurally higher for India than China: India's Himalayan terrain is steep, landslide-prone, and snowed in during winter with north-south ridgelines that make lateral troop movement difficult, while China's Tibetan-plateau side is flat and allows roads to be built close and parallel to the border across its length.
insights.speaker: Saheb Singh Chadha
insights.so_what: A durable geographic asymmetry that structurally favors China in any renewed border standoff — a reason India's military buildup and infrastructure spending at the border is likely to remain a permanent high-cost commitment rather than a temporary crisis response.
insights.locator: On the Indian side, the Himalayan terrain is steep
